content:
# Numbers Chapter 20 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 172-173 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_020_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_020_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0127-0128

---

**20:1** And the Sons of Israel, the whole congregation, came into the wilderness of Zin in the first month, and the people stayed in Kadesh. And Miriam died there and was buried there.

**20:2** And there was no water for the congregation, and they assembled against Moses and against Aaron.

**20:3** And the people contended with Moses and spoke, saying, "If only we had perished when our brothers perished before YHWH!

**20:4** And why have you brought the assembly of YHWH into this wilderness, to die there -- we and our livestock?

**20:5** And why have you brought us up from Egypt, to bring us to this evil place? It is not a place of seed, or of figs, or of vines, or of pomegranates; and there is no water to drink."

**20:6** And Moses and Aaron went from the presence of the assembly to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, and they fell upon their faces. And the glory of YHWH appeared to them.

**20:7** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**20:8** "Take the staff and assemble the congregation -- you and Aaron your brother -- and speak to the rock before their eyes, and it shall give its water. And you shall bring forth water from the rock for them, and you shall give drink to the congregation and their livestock."

**20:9** And Moses took the staff from before YHWH, as he had commanded him.

**20:10** And Moses and Aaron assembled the assembly before the rock. And he said to them, "Hear now, you rebels! Shall we bring forth water for you from this rock?"

**20:11** And Moses raised his hand and struck the rock with his staff twice. And water came out abundantly, and the congregation drank, and their livestock.

**20:12** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron, "Because you did not believe in me, to sanctify me before the eyes of the Sons of Israel, therefore you shall not bring this assembly into the land which I have given to them."

**20:13** These are the waters of Meribah, where the Sons of Israel contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them.

**20:14** And Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom: "Thus says your brother Israel: You know all the hardship that has found us --

**20:15** that our fathers went down to Egypt, and we dwelt in Egypt many days, and the Egyptians dealt evil with us and with our fathers.

**20:16** And we cried out to YHWH, and he heard our voice, and he sent a messenger and brought us out from Egypt. And behold, we are in Kadesh, a city at the edge of your border.

**20:17** Let us pass, please, through your land. We will not pass through field or through vineyard, and we will not drink water from a well. By the King's Highway we will go; we will not turn to the right or to the left, until we have passed through your border."

**20:18** And Edom said to him, "You shall not pass through me, lest I come out with the sword to meet you."

**20:19** And the Sons of Israel said to him, "By the highway we will go up, and if we drink of your water, I and my livestock, then I will pay its price. Only -- it is nothing -- let me pass through on my feet."

**20:20** And he said, "You shall not pass through." And Edom came out to meet him with a heavy people and with a strong hand.

**20:21** And Edom refused to give Israel passage through his border. And Israel turned away from him.

**20:22** And they set out from Kadesh. And the Sons of Israel, the whole congregation, came to Mount Hor.

**20:23** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron at Mount Hor, on the border of the land of Edom, saying:

**20:24** "Aaron shall be gathered to his people, for he shall not enter the land which I have given to the Sons of Israel, because you rebelled against my mouth at the waters of Meribah.

**20:25** Take Aaron and Eleazar his son, and bring them up to Mount Hor.

**20:26** And strip Aaron of his garments, and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron shall be gathered and shall die there."

**20:27** And Moses did as YHWH commanded, and they went up to Mount Hor before the eyes of all the congregation.

**20:28** And Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron died there on the top of the mountain. And Moses and Eleazar came down from the mountain.

**20:29** And all the congregation saw that Aaron had perished, and they wept for Aaron thirty days, all the house of Israel.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Overall Assessment
All three translators -- EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA -- worked faithfully from the Leningrad Codex (pages 172-173, confirmed in the manuscript images) and cross-referenced the Codex Vaticanus (pages 127-128). The Leningrad Codex presents Numbers 20 across two pages in three-column format, beginning on page 172 (left column, following the conclusion of chapter 19) and continuing onto page 173. The Vaticanus preserves the chapter across pages 127-128, with the section division visible in the second column of page 127. A closed section marker (samekh) precedes verse 1 in the Leningrad Codex, and an open section marker (pe) appears before verse 14, separating the Meribah narrative from the Edom embassy. All three translators correctly identify these parashah divisions.

The chapter presents a chiastic structure framed by two deaths -- Miriam at the opening (v. 1) and Aaron at the close (vv. 22-29) -- with the crisis at the waters of Meribah at its center (vv. 2-13) and the embassy to Edom as a hinge (vv. 14-21). Agreement among the translators is substantial; the disagreements that arise concern register, the handling of Hebrew idiom, and the degree of interpretive latitude in key theological phrases.

### Key Decisions

1. **Verse 1 -- "stayed" vs. "dwelt" vs. "settled":** EMET uses the literal gloss "dwelt" (va-yeshev); LOGOS renders "stayed"; PNEUMA renders "settled." SOPHIA selects "stayed" (LOGOS) as the most natural English rendering for a temporary encampment, while noting that "dwelt" and "settled" both have legitimate claims on the Hebrew root yashav.

2. **Verse 6 -- "Tent of Meeting" (capitalization):** EMET renders "Tent of Meeting" with full capitalization; LOGOS renders "tent of meeting" in lowercase; PNEUMA renders "Tent of Meeting." SOPHIA follows the capitalized form as a proper noun designating the specific sanctuary structure, consistent with standard English convention for sacred objects.

3. **Verse 8 -- word order in the provision clause:** EMET renders closely to Hebrew word order: "you shall bring out for them water from the rock"; LOGOS renders "you shall bring forth for them water from the rock"; PNEUMA renders "You will bring water out of the rock for them." SOPHIA follows LOGOS with a minor adjustment for clarity: "you shall bring forth water from the rock for them" -- preserving formal register while achieving readable English word order.

4. **Verse 15 -- "dealt evil with us" vs. "did evil to us" vs. "treated us badly":** The Hebrew va-yare'u lanu is rendered differently by each translator. EMET: "dealt evil to us"; LOGOS: "did evil to us"; PNEUMA: "treated us badly." SOPHIA selects "dealt evil with us" -- preserving the formal register and the Hebrew sense of ra'a (evil/harm) while slightly adjusting the preposition for English idiom.

5. **Verse 16 -- "messenger" (mal'akh):** All three translators render mal'akh as "messenger" rather than "angel," correctly preserving the ambiguity of the Hebrew term in this diplomatic context. SOPHIA concurs.

6. **Verse 21 -- "passage" vs. "to pass":** EMET renders the infinitive construct literally: "to give Israel to pass through his border." LOGOS renders "to give Israel passage through his border." PNEUMA renders "to allow Israel to pass through his territory." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "to give Israel passage through his border" -- formal and clear.

7. **Verse 24 -- "rebelled against my mouth" vs. "rebelled against my command":** EMET and LOGOS retain the literal "rebelled against my mouth" (meritem et-pi); PNEUMA renders interpretively as "rebelled against my command." SOPHIA retains "rebelled against my mouth" as the more faithful and evocative rendering, preserving the Hebrew idiom that connects speech (the divine "mouth") to the command Moses failed to obey (speaking to the rock rather than striking it).

8. **Verse 27 -- "as YHWH commanded" vs. "as YHWH had commanded":** EMET and LOGOS render "as YHWH commanded" (simple past); PNEUMA renders "as YHWH had commanded" (pluperfect). The Hebrew ka'asher tsivvah YHWH is a simple perfect. SOPHIA follows the simple past: "as YHWH commanded."

---

## Disagreement Log

### 20:1 -- "stayed" vs. "dwelt" vs. "settled" (va-yeshev)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "dwelt" |
| LOGOS | "stayed" |
| PNEUMA | "settled" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "stayed" -- the most natural English term for a temporary wilderness encampment. The Hebrew yashav covers a range from temporary sojourn to permanent settlement; in the wilderness context, "stayed" best captures the transience.

### 20:3 -- "contended" vs. "quarreled" and "Would that we had perished" vs. "If only we had perished"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "contended ... And if only we had expired" |
| LOGOS | "contended ... Would that we had perished" |
| PNEUMA | "quarreled ... If only we had perished" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "contended" (EMET/LOGOS) -- preserving the connection to the root riv that names the place Meribah ("contention") in verse 13. "If only we had perished" -- the optative "if only" is more natural in modern English than "would that," while "perished" is preferred over "expired" as clearer in English without losing the gravity of the Hebrew gava.

### 20:4 -- "to die there, we and our livestock" vs. "for us and our livestock to die here"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to die there, we and our livestock" |
| LOGOS | "to die there, we and our livestock" |
| PNEUMA | "for us and our livestock to die here" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to die there -- we and our livestock" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew lamut sham anakhnu u-ve'irenu places the infinitive "to die" first with the appositional "we and our livestock" following, which the EMET/LOGOS rendering preserves. PNEUMA's restructuring, while clear, loses the Hebrew syntax.

### 20:5 -- "evil place" vs. "terrible place"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the evil place" |
| LOGOS | "this evil place" |
| PNEUMA | "this terrible place" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "this evil place" -- the Hebrew ra' means "evil, bad, wretched." "Evil" preserves the Hebrew register and the theological resonance of the term; "terrible" is too interpretive.

### 20:5 -- "seed" vs. "grain"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "seed" |
| LOGOS | "seed" |
| PNEUMA | "grain" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "seed" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew zera means "seed" and refers to sown crops generally, not grain specifically. "Seed" preserves the broader meaning and the literal Hebrew.

### 20:8 -- "it shall give its water" vs. "it will yield its water"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "it shall give its waters" |
| LOGOS | "it shall give its water" |
| PNEUMA | "it will yield its water" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "it shall give its water" (LOGOS). The Hebrew ve-natan meimav uses the simple verb natan ("to give"); "yield" (PNEUMA) is interpretive. The singular "water" (LOGOS) follows standard English usage; EMET's plural "waters" reflects the Hebrew plural meimav but is unusual in English for this context.

### 20:10 -- "Hear now" vs. "Listen now"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Hear now" |
| LOGOS | "Hear now" |
| PNEUMA | "Listen now" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Hear now" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew shim'u-na is the imperative of shama ("to hear"). "Hear" is the more literal and formal rendering; "listen" is functionally equivalent but less precise.

### 20:11 -- "twice" vs. "two times" and "came out abundantly" vs. "came gushing out abundantly"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "twice; and came out water abundant" |
| LOGOS | "twice. And water came out abundantly" |
| PNEUMA | "two times. And water came gushing out abundantly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "twice" (EMET/LOGOS) -- the standard English equivalent for pa'amayim. "And water came out abundantly" (LOGOS) -- the Hebrew mayim rabbim means simply "abundant/much water"; "gushing" (PNEUMA) is an interpretive addition not present in the Hebrew.

### 20:12 -- "believe in me" vs. "trust in me" and "to sanctify me before the eyes of" vs. "to treat me as holy before the eyes of"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you did not believe in me, to sanctify me before the eyes of" |
| LOGOS | "you did not believe in me, to sanctify me in the eyes of" |
| PNEUMA | "you did not trust in me -- to treat me as holy before the eyes of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you did not believe in me, to sanctify me before the eyes of" (EMET). The Hebrew he'emantem is the hiphil of 'aman, and "believe" is the traditional and etymologically precise rendering (the root of emunah, "faith"). "Trust" (PNEUMA) is semantically close but loses the specific biblical resonance. "To sanctify me" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "to treat me as holy" (PNEUMA), which is a paraphrase. "Before the eyes of" (EMET/PNEUMA) better represents le-einei than "in the eyes of" (LOGOS).

### 20:13 -- "contended" vs. "quarreled" and "he was sanctified" vs. "he showed himself holy"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them" |
| LOGOS | "contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them" |
| PNEUMA | "quarreled with YHWH, and he showed himself holy among them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them" (EMET/LOGOS). "Contended" preserves the riv root connection to "Meribah." The niphal va-yiqqadesh is best rendered as the passive "he was sanctified" rather than the reflexive "he showed himself holy," which is interpretive.

### 20:14 -- "Thus says" vs. "This is what ... says"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Thus says your brother Israel" |
| LOGOS | "Thus says your brother Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "This is what your brother Israel says" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Thus says your brother Israel" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew messenger formula koh amar is a fixed diplomatic and prophetic convention best rendered with the traditional "thus says."

### 20:15 -- "many days" vs. "a long time"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "days many" |
| LOGOS | "many days" |
| PNEUMA | "a long time" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "many days" (LOGOS). The Hebrew yamim rabbim literally means "many days." PNEUMA's "a long time" is a paraphrase that loses the specific idiom.

### 20:15 -- "dealt evil" vs. "did evil" vs. "treated us badly"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "dealt evil to us, Egypt, and to our fathers" |
| LOGOS | "the Egyptians did evil to us and to our fathers" |
| PNEUMA | "the Egyptians treated us badly -- us and our fathers" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Egyptians dealt evil with us and with our fathers." SOPHIA adjusts the subject from "Egypt" (EMET, following the Hebrew personification) to "the Egyptians" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for English clarity, while preserving "dealt evil" over "did evil" or "treated badly" for formal register.

### 20:17 -- "water of a well" vs. "water from a well" vs. "water from any well"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "water of a well" |
| LOGOS | "water of a well" |
| PNEUMA | "water from any well" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "water from a well" -- adjusting EMET/LOGOS's literal "of" to "from" for English idiom, while rejecting PNEUMA's addition of "any," which is not in the Hebrew.

### 20:18 -- "lest I come out with the sword to meet you" vs. "or I will come out against you with the sword"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "lest with the sword I go out to meet you" |
| LOGOS | "lest I come out with the sword to meet you" |
| PNEUMA | "or I will come out against you with the sword" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "lest I come out with the sword to meet you" (LOGOS). The Hebrew pen introduces a purpose/fear clause best rendered "lest." PNEUMA's "or" is too casual for the formal diplomatic threat.

### 20:20 -- "heavy people" vs. "large fighting force"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "with a people heavy and with a hand strong" |
| LOGOS | "with a heavy people and with a strong hand" |
| PNEUMA | "with a large fighting force and a strong hand" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "with a heavy people and with a strong hand" (LOGOS). The Hebrew am kaved literally means "a heavy/numerous people." PNEUMA's "large fighting force" is interpretive and loses the important Hebrew adjective kaved ("heavy"), which carries deliberate echoes of Pharaoh's hardened (kaved) heart and YHWH's strong hand in the Exodus narrative.

### 20:24 -- "shall be gathered" vs. "is to be gathered" and "my mouth" vs. "my command"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Shall be gathered Aaron ... you rebelled against my mouth" |
| LOGOS | "Aaron shall be gathered ... you rebelled against my mouth" |
| PNEUMA | "Aaron is to be gathered ... you both rebelled against my command" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Aaron shall be gathered ... you rebelled against my mouth" (LOGOS). See Council Note 7 above regarding "my mouth" vs. "my command." The addition of "both" (PNEUMA) is not explicit in the Hebrew, though the verb meritem is indeed plural.

### 20:26 -- "shall be gathered and shall die" vs. "will be gathered ... and will die"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shall be gathered and shall die" |
| LOGOS | "shall be gathered and shall die" |
| PNEUMA | "will be gathered ... and will die" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "shall be gathered and shall die" (EMET/LOGOS). The formal "shall" preserves the gravity of divine decree; "will" (PNEUMA) reduces the solemnity.

### 20:29 -- "perished" vs. "expired" vs. "died" (gava)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "had expired" |
| LOGOS | "had perished" |
| PNEUMA | "had died" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "had perished" (LOGOS). The Hebrew gava is a marked term meaning "to breathe one's last, expire, perish" -- distinct from the common mut ("to die"). "Perished" preserves the distinctiveness of the term and its echo of the people's wish in verse 3 (lu gava'nu, "if only we had perished"), while being clearer in English than "expired" (EMET). PNEUMA's "died" erases the distinction between gava and mut.

### 20:29 -- "wept for" vs. "mourned for"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "wept for Aaron" |
| LOGOS | "wept for Aaron" |
| PNEUMA | "mourned for Aaron" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "wept for Aaron" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew va-yivku is specifically "they wept" from the root bakah ("to weep, cry"), not a general term for mourning. "Wept" preserves the visceral, physical image of communal grief.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 172-173, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pages 127-128. YHWH is transliterated, not translated. Conjunctions are preserved. Numbers rendered in English words. "Sons of Israel" is maintained throughout.*

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter*
